It looks like this is happening because, in your `process_audio_file_with_spotify_search` function, you first rename the file and then try to embed album art into it using the original file path. By the time you attempt to embed the album art, the file has already been renamed, so the original path (`audio_file_path`) no longer points to an existing file. To resolve this issue, you need to ensure that the operations on the file (such as embedding album art) are completed before you rename the file. Alternatively, if operations need to be done after renaming, use the new file path (`new_file_path`) for those operations. ### Revised Approach: 1. **Rearrange Operations**: Make sure all operations that need the original file are completed before renaming. 2. **Use Updated File Path**: If there are operations to be done after the file has been renamed, use the updated file path for those. This approach will prevent the `OSError` by avoiding attempts to interact with a non-existent file path after renaming.

np.max(np.abs(signal)) def generate_triangle_signal(length, frequency, amplitude): t = np.arange(length) signal = amplitude * (2 * np.abs((t / length - 0.5) % (1 / frequency) - 0.5) - 0.5) return signal / np.max(np.abs(signal)) def generate_ramp_signal(length, slope): t = np.arange(length) signal = slope * (t / length) return signal / np.max(np.abs(signal)) def generate_impulse_signal(length, positions): signal = np.zeros(length) signal[positions] = 1 return signal def generate_random_noise_signal(length, amplitude_range): signal = np.random.uniform(amplitude_range[0], amplitude_range[1], length) return signal / np.max(np.abs(signal)) def generate_constant_signal(length, constant_value): signal = np.full(length, constant_value) return signal def generate_signals(N, length, frequencies, triangle_amplitude, ramp_slopes, impulse_positions, noise_amplitude_range, constant_values): signals = [] for _ in range(N): sin_signal = generate_sin_signal(length, np.random.choice(frequencies)) triangle_signal = generate_triangle_signal(length, np.random.choice(frequencies), triangle_amplitude) ramp_signal = generate_ramp_signal(length, np.random.choice(ramp_slopes)) impulse_signal = 0.5) % (1 / frequency) - 0.5) - 0.5) return signal / np.max(np.abs(signal)) def generate_ramp_signal(length, slope): t = np.arange(length) signal = slope * (t / length) return signal / np.max(np.abs(signal)) def generate_impulse_signal(length, positions): signal = np.zeros(length) signal[positions] = 1 return signal def generate_random_noise_signal(length, amplitude_range): signal = np.random.uniform(amplitude_range[0], amplitude_range[1], length) return signal / np.max(np.abs(signal)) def generate_constant_signal(length, constant_value): signal = np.full(length, constant_value) return signal def generate_signals(N, length, frequencies, triangle_amplitude, ramp_slopes, impulse_positions, noise_amplitude_range, constant_values): signals = [] for _ in range(N): sin_signal = generate_sin_signal(length, np.random.choice(frequencies)) triangle_signal = generate_triangle_signal(length, np.random.choice(frequencies), triangle_amplitude) ramp_signal = generate_ramp_signal(length, np.random.choice(ramp_slopes)) impulse_signal = generate_impulse_signal(length, np.random.choice(impulse_positions)) noise_signal = generate_random_noise_signal(length, noise_amplitude_range) constant_signal = generate_constant_signal(length, np.random.choice(constant_values)) stacked_signal = np.vstack((sin_signal, triangle_signal, ramp_signal, impulse_signal, noise_signal, constant_signal)) signals.append(stacked_signal) return signals def plot_signals(signals, titles, num_random_signals): num_signals = len(signals) num_rows = signals[0].shape[0] num_plots = min(num_signals, num_random_signals) fig, axs = plt.subplots(num_plots, num_rows, figsize=(12, 8)) fig.tight_layout() for i in range(num_plots): for j in range(num_rows): axs[i, j].plot(signals[i][j]) axs[i, j].set_ylim(-1, 1) axs[i, j].set_xticks([]) axs[i, j].set_yticks([-1, 0, 1]) if i == 0: axs[i, j].set_title(titles[j]) if j == 0: axs[i, j].set_ylabel(f"Signal {i + 1}") plt.show()

LLM: Creating a Photoshop script that accomplishes this can be quite complex. Below is a script that should help get you started. This script uses JavaScript and is intended to be run from within the Adobe Photoshop Scripting environment. This script creates a user interface with the specified elements, allows the user to select up to two image files, places them into the document and renames the layers based on the conditions you provided. ```javascript #target photoshop // Make sure we are using Photoshop if (app.name !== "Adobe Photoshop") { alert("This script is only compatible with Adobe Photoshop."); exit(); } // Create a dialog window var dialog = new Window("dialog", "Wall Image Importer"); // Add input field dialog.add("statictext", undefined, "Enter 4D Wall Name:"); var wallNameField = dialog.add("edittext", undefined, ""); wallNameField.characters = 20; // Function to select files and place them into the document function selectAndPlaceFiles() { var files = File.openDialog("Select the Titled and Untitled Versions of Wall", "*.jpg; *.jpeg; *.psd", true); if (files && files.length <= 2) { for (var i = 0; i < files.length; i++) { var file = files[i]; var fileName = file.name.toUpperCase(); var layerName; if (fileName.includes("TITLE")) { layerName = "Titled Version"; } else if (fileName.includes("NO TITLE") || (!fileName.includes("TITLE") && !fileName.includes("NOTITLE"))) { layerName = "Untitled Version"; } app.open(file); var importedDoc = app.activeDocument; // Duplicate the layer to the original document importedDoc.artLayers[0].duplicate(app.documents[0]); importedDoc.close(SaveOptions.DONOTSAVECHANGES); app.activeDocument.activeLayer.name = layerName; } } else { alert("Please select up to two image files."); } } // Add "Select Files" button with description var selectFilesGroup = dialog.add("group"); selectFilesGroup.orientation = "row"; var selectFilesButton = selectFilesGroup.add("button", undefined, "Select Files"); selectFilesGroup.add("statictext", undefined, "Select the Titled and Untitled Versions of Wall"); selectFilesButton.onClick = function() { selectAndPlaceFiles(); } // Add OK and Cancel buttons dialog.add("button", undefined, "OK"); dialog.add("button", undefined, "Cancel"); // Show the dialog dialog.center(); dialog.show(); ``` To run this script: 1. Copy the above code to a plain text editor and save it with a `.jsx` extension. 2. Open Adobe Photoshop. 3. Go to `File` > `Scripts` > `Browse...` and select the script file you saved. 4. The user interface should appear, and you can begin using the tool. Once the user clicks "Select Files", the script allows the selection of up to two files (as per the constraints you specified). After the files are selected, the script will open them, check the filenames to determine if they are titled or untitled, duplicate the layers to the working document, and rename the layers accordingly. **Note:** The script doesn't handle the "4D Wall Name." If you want this to be used for folder naming or saving files, you would need to add additional functionality to do something with that input. This script simply lays out the basics of the UI and functionality you requested.

When executed, the "Run" button triggers the MAIN function, which imports the selected files (maximum of 2, and only in PSD or JPG format) into the currently open Photoshop document and processes their names to remove the specified substrings. ```javascript #target photoshop if (app.documents.length === 0) { alert("No document is open. Please open a document before running this script."); exit(); } var selectedFiles; var mainTitles = []; // MAIN function that places files and processes layer names function MAIN(filePaths, fileTitles) { for (var i = 0; i < filePaths.length; i++) { var openedFile = open(filePaths[i]); // Duplicate the opened file's layer into the current active document openedFile.activeLayer.duplicate(app.activeDocument); openedFile.close(SaveOptions.DONOTSAVECHANGES); // Rename the duplicated layer with the processed title var currentDoc = app.activeDocument; var duplicatedLayer = currentDoc.activeLayer; duplicatedLayer.name = fileTitles[i]; } } // Create dialog window for user interface var dialog = new Window('dialog', 'Import Files and Process Titles'); dialog.orientation = 'column'; dialog.alignChildren = 'left'; // Add 'Select Files' button var selectFilesButton = dialog.add('button', undefined, 'Select Files...'); selectFilesButton.onClick = function() { selectedFiles = File.openDialog('Select up to 2 files (PSD or JPG)', 'Photoshop:*.psd;JPEG:*.jpg', true); if (selectedFiles && selectedFiles.length > 2) { alert('Please select up to 2 files only.'); selectedFiles = null; // Reset the selection } }; // Add 'Run' button var runButton = dialog.add('button', undefined, 'Run'); runButton.onClick = function() { if (!selectedFiles) { alert("Please select files before running."); return; } // Process file names and store in mainTitles for (var i = 0; i < selectedFiles.length; i++) { var file = selectedFiles[i]; var title = file.name .replace(/4 D /gi, '') .replace(/TITLE/gi, '') .replace(/NO TITLE/gi, '') .replace(/\.[^\.]*$/, ''); // Remove file extension mainTitles.push(title); } dialog.close(); MAIN(selectedFiles, mainTitles); }; // Add 'Cancel' button dialog.add('button', undefined, 'Cancel'); // Show the dialog window dialog.show(); ``` When the script is run, a dialog window is displayed prompting the user to select files (two at max, either PSD or JPG). Upon clicking the "Run" button, the script analyzes the selected file names, eliminates the defined substrings ('4 D ', 'TITLE', 'NO TITLE'), and proceeds to import the files as new layers into the active document, assigning them the processed names. Please follow these steps to execute the script in Photoshop: 1. Open Photoshop and ensure that a document is open to receive the imported layers. 2. Copy the script into a plain text editor and save the file with a `.jsx` extension. 3. Navigate to `File` > `Scripts` > `Browse...` in Photoshop, then locate and run the saved JSX file. 4. USER: I want you to implement deletion from a trie. you can assume that our strings only consist of lower-case letters. A friend has already written the following piece of code and it’s all correctly implemented. struct TrieNode { bool isInSet; // true if there is a word ending at this node. TrieNode *parent; // the parent in the trie, NULL if the node is the root. TrieNode *child[26]; // the (up to) 26 children for the letters. NULL if there is no such child. int convert (char letter) // turns a letter into its index in the child array { return (int) (letter - ’a’); } bool isLeaf () { // returns whether the node is a leaf for (int i = 0; i < 26; i ++) if (child[i] != NULL) return false; return true; } }; class InsertOnlyTrie { protected: TrieNode *root; public: InsertOnlyTrie () { root = NULL; } ~InsertOnlyTrie (); // correctly implemented destructor void add (string s); // adds the string to the trie correctly. Is already implemented. TrieNode *lookup (string s); /* returns the pointer to the node at which the search for s terminates in the trie. Is already implemented. If s is not in the trie, returns NULL. */ }; you should use your friend’s class to build a “real” Trie class that also provides the following public function. void remove (string s); /* If s is not in the trie, the function should not do anything. Otherwise, it should remove s from the trie as though it had never been added. */

LLM: Below, I'll provide the implementation of the derived class `Trie`, which extends the `InsertOnlyTrie` class. It will include the implementation of the `remove` function to enable deletion of a string from the trie. We start by walking down the trie to locate the node that marks the end of the string we want to delete. If the string exists, we mark the `isInSet` flag as `false`. Then, we traverse back up the trie (using the `parent` pointer), deleting nodes if they are leaves and no other word depends on them. Here is the complete implementation of the `Trie` class and the `remove` method: ```cpp #include <string> using namespace std; class Trie : public InsertOnlyTrie { public: // Constructor and Destructor remain as is (inherited from InsertOnlyTrie). Trie() : InsertOnlyTrie() {} void remove(string s) { // Step 1: Look up the target node for the string. TrieNode* endNode = lookup(s); if (endNode == NULL || !endNode->isInSet) { // The string s is not in the trie, so we do nothing. return; } // Step 2: Mark the endNode as no longer being the end of a word. endNode->isInSet = false; // Step 3: Remove unnecessary leaf nodes while traversing upward. TrieNode* current = endNode; while (current != root && current->isLeaf() && !current->isInSet) { TrieNode* parent = current->parent; // Find the index of the current node in its parent's child array. for (int i = 0; i < 26; i++) { if (parent->child[i] == current) { parent->child[i] = NULL; break; } } // Delete the current node. delete current; // Move to the parent node. current = parent; } } }; ``` ### Explanation of the `remove` method: 1. **Locate the Node**: - We use the `lookup` method to find the node where the search for the string `s` ends. If `s` is not found, or the node's `isInSet` flag is already `false`, this means the string isn't in the trie. In this case, we don't need to do anything and return immediately. 2. **Unmark the Node**: - If the string exists in the trie, we unmark it by setting `endNode->isInSet = false`. This ensures that the string no longer represents a word in the trie. 3. **Cleanup Unused Nodes**: - Starting from the `endNode`, we traverse upward through the `parent` pointers. - We check if the current node is a leaf (using `isLeaf()`) and if no other string depends on this node (`isInSet == false`). - If the conditions are met, it means the node is redundant, so we: - Update the parent's `child` pointer to set the current node to `NULL`. - Delete the current node. - Move up to the parent and repeat the process until we reach the root or a node that cannot be deleted because it's part of another word. ### Key Points: - The `isInSet` flag ensures we don't remove nodes required for other strings in the trie. - The cleanup process stops as soon as we encounter a node that is not a leaf or is still part of another word. - Deletion is efficient since it only removes nodes that are no longer necessary. ### Example Usage: Suppose the trie contains the strings `{"hello", "help", "helping"}`: 1. After calling `remove("help")`, only the path for `"helping"` remains after `"hel"`, while `"hello"` is still intact. 2. After calling `remove("hello")`, the path for `"h", "e", "l", "l", "o"` is removed if and only if no other word depends on it. This implementation ensures correctness by leveraging the existing `InsertOnlyTrie` class and carefully managing the trie structure during deletion.

LLM: Let's analyze each problem and propose a well-suited data structure to address the task: --- ### **(a) Emergency Room:** **Task:** We need to identify the patient with the most life-threatening injury in constant time and be able to quickly add new patients to the queue. **Proposed Data Structure:** A **priority queue (or binary heap)** is an ideal data structure for this problem. **Reasoning:** - Priority queues allow us to retrieve the patient with the highest priority (most life-threatening injury) in **O(1)** time or **O(log n)** if implemented using a binary heap. - Adding a new patient requires **O(log n)** time if using a binary heap. - You can store patients as objects where their priority (injury severity) is the key. - If priorities require frequent updates (e.g., patient condition worsens), consider using a **Fibonacci heap**, as it supports decreases in priority in **O(1)** time. **Modifications:** None required for a standard priority queue, but consider advanced variants like Fibonacci heaps if injury severity can change. --- ### **(b) Air-Traffic Controller:** **Task:** Efficiently track which landing strip each airplane is on, where airplanes have 6-character alphanumeric IDs. We need strong worst-case running time guarantees. **Proposed Data Structure:** A **hash table** with perfect hashing or a **balanced binary search tree (e.g., AVL or Red-Black Tree)**. **Reasoning:** 1. **Hash Table**: - Hash tables allow for **O(1)** average-case insertion, deletion, and lookup times. - To guarantee worst-case constant lookup, use **perfect hashing,** which constructs a collision-free hash function. This ensures **O(1)** worst-case lookups. 2. **Balanced Binary Search Tree (e.g., AVL/Red-Black Tree)**: - If we require a guarantee of logarithmic time for all operations in the worst case, a balanced BST ensures **O(log n)** for insertion, deletion, and search. **Modifications:** - If using a hash table: - Use a custom hash function to ensure good key distribution. - If collisions are unacceptable, implement perfect hashing or use a large-enough hash table. - If using a balanced BST: - Store planes by their unique alphanumeric IDs and associate each ID with the landing strip. **Tradeoff:** Choose a hash table for fast average-case performance or a balanced BST if worst-case performance is critical. --- ### **(c) Database of PGP Cryptosystem Keys:** **Task:** We need to manage up to 500 million keys. Each key is a string of 400 characters, and we need to detect duplicates on insertion. The memory available is only 2GB. **Proposed Data Structure:** A **Bloom Filter** coupled with **secondary storage (disk-based database)**. **Reasoning:** - PGP public keys can be stored on external storage (e.g., disk) for long-term storage. - A **Bloom Filter** is a space-efficient probabilistic data structure that can quickly check if a key *might* already exist, with a very small false positive rate. This avoids the need to load the entire database into memory. - The Bloom Filter supports up to 500 million entries with a low false positive rate within a small memory footprint (<2GB). **Modifications/Workflow:** 1. Use a 2-level system: - **Bloom Filter (RAM):** To quickly check if the key might exist. If the Bloom Filter returns `false`, the key does not exist, and we can safely add it. - **External Storage (Disk):** To store the actual keys. When a Bloom Filter returns `true`, perform a secondary disk lookup to resolve false positives. 2. Tune the Bloom Filter size and hash functions to maintain a low false positive rate (e.g., 1%). 3. Compress or use hashing for the 400-character keys when storing them (optional). **Tradeoffs:** - Bloom Filters have false positives, but we mitigate this with a secondary disk check. - Memory usage is kept under 2GB. --- ### **(d) Templated Map:** **Task:** We need a templated map that supports insert, remove, and find with: - Average-case time complexity: **O(1)**. - Worst-case time complexity: **O(log n)**. **Proposed Data Structure:** A **combination of a hash table and a balanced binary search tree (e.g., AVL or Red-Black Tree)**. **Reasoning:** 1. **Hash Table:** - A hash table ensures **O(1)** average-case time for insertion, deletion, and find operations in most cases. - Collisions can occur, which degrade performance. 2. **Balanced Binary Search Tree:** - Since we require **O(log n)** worst-case performance, a balanced binary search tree can handle worst-case operations. - Implementation handles collisions by default, as nodes can represent hash collisions. **Plan:** - **Hybrid Approach**: Store the map in a hash table for average-case performance, but build a balanced binary search tree to resolve hash collisions. In the event of a collision, operations fallback to the tree. - Use the hash table for fast access under normal conditions. - For templates, use the C++ STL `std::unordered_map` for the hash table and `std::map` for collision management within the hybrid approach. **Modifications:** - To implement the hybrid data structure: - Include a balanced binary search tree per hash bucket to handle collisions. - If worst-case conditions are rare, this hybrid solution performs like a hash table in the average case but retains the tree's guarantees in the worst case. --- ### Summary of Chosen Data Structures: | **Task** | **Recommended Data Structure** | **Reasoning** | |----------|----------------------------|--------------| | (a) Emergency Room | **Priority Queue (Heap)** | Fast retrieval of the highest-priority patient and efficient insertion | | (b) Air-Traffic Controller | **Hash Table (Perfect Hashing)** or **Balanced BST** | Strong time guarantees for worst-case insert, delete, and search | | (c) PGP Key Database | **Bloom Filter + External Storage** | Space efficiency for detecting duplicates and supporting large keys | | (d) Templated Map | **Hybrid: Hash Table + Balanced BST** | O(1) average-case operations with O(log n) worst-case guarantees | Let me know if you'd like further clarification!

LLM: To generate a PDF on the client-side instead of sending HTML to the server, you can use libraries such as `jsPDF` or `html2pdf.js`. These libraries allow you to convert HTML content directly to a PDF file in the client browser. Here's how you can achieve this using `html2pdf.js`, which is built on top of `jsPDF` and simplifies the process: 1. **Install the library:** You can install `html2pdf.js` via npm or add it directly through a CDN. If you are using npm, run the following command: ```bash npm install html2pdf.js ``` Or, include it via a CDN in your HTML (usually in the `index.html`): ```html <script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/html2pdf.js"></script> ``` 2. **Modify Your Code to Use the Library:** Update your `handlePDFDownload` function to use the `html2pdf.js` library for generating the PDF. Here's how you can do it: ```javascript async function handlePDFDownload(element) { if (!element.current) return; try { // Use html2pdf.js to generate and download the PDF const pdfOptions = { margin: 10, filename: 'schedule.pdf', image: { type: 'jpeg', quality: 0.98 }, html2canvas: { scale: 2 }, jsPDF: { unit: 'mm', format: 'a4', orientation: 'portrait' } }; await html2pdf().from(element.current).set(pdfOptions).save(); } catch (error) { console.error("Error downloading PDF:", error); } } ``` This code utilizes `html2pdf.js` to convert the DOM element (`element.current`) into a PDF with specific configurations, such as margins and format, and then triggers the download in the user's browser. 3. **Ensure your component is properly wrapped within an HTML element:** Make sure that the DOM element you are converting to a PDF is well-structured and doesn't contain CSS styling conflicts that could interfere with rendering in the PDF. The styling should be compatible with what `html2canvas` can render. 4. **Check Browser Compatibility:** Make sure to test your implementation on the browsers you need to support, as some PDF-generation features might behave differently on different platforms. With `html2pdf.js`, the PDF generation is efficiently handled on the client-side, reducing server load and improving user experience by instantly providing the PDF file for download.

LLM: The error you're encountering (`yii\web\BadRequestHttpException: Unable to verify your data submission`) often arises in Yii2 when the application is unable to validate the CSRF token sent with the form request. Here are some steps you can take to resolve this issue, especially when dealing with an API backend and a frontend built in React. ### Step 1: Ensure CSRF Protection is Correctly Handled 1. **CSRF Token Configuration**: Make sure that CSRF validation is enabled in your Yii2 application configuration. You can do this usually in `config/web.php`: ```php 'params' => [ ... ], 'components' => [ 'request' => [ 'class' => 'yii\web\Request', 'enableCsrfValidation' => true, 'csrfParam' => '_csrf', // Default value; customize if necessary ... ], ... ], ``` 2. **Handle CSRF Token in API Requests**: Since you are using a React frontend, you need to make sure that the CSRF token is sent in each request that modifies state (like a POST request). You can do this by including the CSRF token in your headers. In your Yii2 controller, you can expose the CSRF token via an endpoint that your React app can hit to retrieve it before making its requests: ```php public function actionGetCsrf() { return [ 'csrfToken' => Yii::$app->request->csrfToken, ]; } ``` Then, in your React app: ```javascript fetch('http://your-yii2-backend-url/get-csrf') .then(response => response.json()) .then(data => { const csrfToken = data.csrfToken; // Use this token in your subsequent API requests fetch('http://your-yii2-backend-url/your-endpoint', { method: 'POST', headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json', 'X-CSRF-Token': csrfToken, }, body: JSON.stringify(formData) }); }); ``` ### Step 2: Handle CORS Make sure your CORS settings allow the requests from your React app. You can configure this in your Yii2 application. For example, you can use the following in your `config/web.php`: ```php 'components' => [ ... 'response' => [ 'format' => yii\web\Response::FORMAT_JSON, 'formatters' => [ yii\web\Response::FORMAT_JSON => [ 'class' => 'yii\web\JsonResponseFormatter', 'prettyPrint' => YII_DEBUG, // Use "pretty" formatting in debug mode 'encodingOptions' => JSON_UNESCAPED_SLASHES | JSON_UNESCAPED_UNICODE, ], ], ], ... ], 'on beforeResponse' => function ($event) { Yii::$app->response->headers->add('Access-Control-Allow-Origin', '*'); // Adjust as needed Yii::$app->response->headers->add('Access-Control-Allow-Methods', 'GET, POST, OPTIONS'); Yii::$app->response->headers->add('Access-Control-Allow-Headers', 'X-CSRF-Token, Content-Type'); }, ``` ### Step 3: Test with Postman Before testing with your React frontend, try using Postman or a similar tool to simulate the API call to your Yii2 backend. Ensure that you include the CSRF token in the headers as well. ### Step 4: Check for Validation Errors If you continue to see the error, ensure that your forms are correctly sending the required fields and that you validate the data on the server side as expected. ### Conclusion By ensuring that CSRF tokens are correctly managed between your React frontend and your Yii2 backend and by appropriately handling CORS, you should be able to resolve the `BadRequestHttpException` and prevent verification issues with your data submissions.

LLM: No, you don't necessarily need to set the response format to `Response::FORMAT_JSON` in every action of your controller if you have already configured the global response format in your application configuration (i.e., in your `config/web.php`). When you set the `'response'` component's format to `yii\web\Response::FORMAT_JSON`, all of your controller actions will default to returning JSON responses unless overridden within the action itself. ### When to Set the Response Format 1. **If All Actions are JSON**: If all or most of your actions will return JSON responses, it is sufficient to just configure it globally. You can simply return data from your actions directly: ```php public function actionSubmitForm() { $model = new YourFormModel(); if ($model->load(Yii::$app->request->post()) && $model->validate()) { // Process your form data here return [ 'success' => true, 'message' => 'Form submitted successfully!', ]; } else { return [ 'success' => false, 'errors' => $model->getErrors(), ]; } } ``` 2. **If You Need Different Formats**: If you have specific actions that need to return a different format (like HTML or XML), then you would set the format for that specific action. For example: ```php public function actionRenderHtml() { \Yii::$app->response->format = Response::FORMAT_HTML; return $this->render('some-view'); // Renders a view instead of returning JSON } ``` ### Summary In short, as long as you have configured your Yii2 application to return JSON globally using the configuration you provided, you don't need to set the response format in each controller action that returns JSON. You can simply return an array, and Yii2 will automatically convert it into a JSON response based on your global configuration.

However, you can use it in a different way. The `encodingOptions` should be set through the `init()` method of the `JsonResponseFormatter` or handled directly when retrieving the format. Here's how you can adjust your configuration in `config/web.php`: ```php 'components' => [ ... 'response' => [ 'format' => yii\web\Response::FORMAT_JSON, 'formatters' => [ yii\web\Response::FORMAT_JSON => [ 'class' => 'yii\web\JsonResponseFormatter', 'prettyPrint' => YII_DEBUG, // Use "pretty" formatting in debug mode // The encodingOptions you want will be added when setting the response. ], ], ], ... ], ``` ### Setting Encoding Options Globally If you want to set specific encoding options for all JSON responses, you will need to set these options directly when you send out the response. This can be done using an event handler for `beforeResponse` or similar logic in your action. Here’s how you can do it: ```php use yii\web\Response; // In your controller action or some specific point where you want to format public function actionSomeAction() { $response = Yii::$app->response; $response->format = Response::FORMAT_JSON; $response->formatters[Response::FORMAT_JSON]->encodingOptions = JSON_UNESCAPED_SLASHES | JSON_UNESCAPED_UNICODE; return [ 'success' => true, 'message' => 'Data processed successfully!' ]; } ``` ### Conclusion 1. Remove the `encodingOptions` from the `formatters` configuration in `config/web.php`. 2. Set `encodingOptions` directly in the controller action just before you return the response. Here's how you can implement it, ensuring that both CSRF tokens and sessions are handled correctly in a Yii2 application. ### Understanding CSRF Tokens 1. **CSRF Protection**: Yii2 generates a CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) token to protect POST requests from being forged. The token is typically stored in the user’s session. 2. **Session Management**: Yii2 uses PHP’s session handling capabilities to manage user sessions. When a user logs in, their session can be used to store user IDs, preferences, and other relevant data. ### Configuration for CSRF with Sessions 1. **Ensure CSRF Validation is Enabled**: In your `config/web.php`, make sure CSRF validation is enabled in the request component: ```php 'components' => [ 'request' => [ 'enableCsrfValidation' => true, 'csrfParam' => '_csrf', // Specify the CSRF token parameter ], ... ], ``` 2. **Generating and Storing CSRF Tokens**: When a form is rendered, a CSRF token is included as a hidden input field in the form. This token is automatically managed by Yii2. Here's how you typically render a form in a view: ```php use yii\helpers\Html; use yii\widgets\ActiveForm; $form = ActiveForm::begin(); ?> field($model, 'fieldName')->textInput() ?>
'btn btn-primary']) ?>
``` ### Handling CSRF in React Frontend For a React frontend that communicates with your Yii2 backend, you would handle CSRF tokens slightly differently: 1. **Fetching CSRF Token**: You may create an API endpoint in your Yii2 app to return the CSRF token to the React app. Here’s an example: ```php public function actionGetCsrf() { return [ 'csrfToken' => \Yii::$app->request->csrfToken, ]; } ``` In your React app, you can fetch this token when the app loads or before making any state-changing requests: ```javascript // Fetch CSRF Token fetch('http://your-yii2-backend-url/get-csrf') .then(response => response.json()) .then(data => { const csrfToken = data.csrfToken; // Store it for later use this.setState({ csrfToken }); }); ``` 2. **Using CSRF Token in API Requests**: When making API POST requests, you need to include the CSRF token in the headers: ```javascript fetch('http://your-yii2-backend-url/submit-form', { method: 'POST', headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json', 'X-CSRF-Token': this.state.csrfToken, // Add the CSRF token here }, body: JSON.stringify(formData), }); ``` ### Conclusion - When using CSRF protection, it's important to ensure that the CSRF token is included in every request that alters server state (POST, PUT, DELETE). - When using sessions, the CSRF token is stored in the user’s session automatically by Yii2. - For a React frontend, retrieve the CSRF token via an API call upon component mount or before any state-changing request and include it in request headers. This approach effectively combines session management and CSRF protection, ensuring your forms are secure and compliant with best practices.

Ensure that you are sending the CSRF token correctly and that the CSRF token is being validated properly in your Yii2 controller. #### Modifications in the React Component In your `handleSubmit` function, ensure that you are including the CSRF token in the request. You had the CSRF token as a hidden input in your form, which isn't strictly necessary for your API request since you can send it directly in the request payload. Here's a corrected version of the `handleSubmit` that includes the CSRF token in the headers if that's how you've set it up: ```javascript const handleSubmit = async (event) => { event.preventDefault(); setError(''); // Clear previous error messages setSuccess(''); // Clear previous success messages console.log('CSRF Token:', csrfToken); // Debugging if (!csrfToken) { console.error('CSRF token is not set.'); setError('CSRF token is not set. Please try again later.'); console.error('Error:', error); } }; ``` ### 2. Validate CSRF Token in the Yii2 Action In your Yii2 controller's `actionSubmitForm` method, ensure that you're validating the CSRF token correctly. You should check for the CSRF token in the headers instead of in the body of the request. Modify your CSRF validation as follows: ```php public function actionSubmitForm(): Array { \Yii::$app->response->format = Response::FORMAT_JSON; $request = Yii::$app->request; \Yii::info('Received POST data: ' . json_encode($request->post()), __METHOD__); // Validate CSRF token from the headers if (!$request->validateCsrfToken($request->headers->get('X-CSRF-Token'))) { \Yii::error('Invalid CSRF token: ' . json_encode($request->headers->get('X-CSRF-Token')), __METHOD__); return [ 'status' => 'error', 'message' => 'Invalid CSRF Token', ]; } $name = $request->post('name'); $email = $request->post('email'); $message = $request->post('message'); \Yii::info("Form submission data - Name: $name, Email: $email, Message: $message", __METHOD__); // Here you can save the data or perform your success logic return [ 'status' => 'success', 'message' => 'Your message has been sent successfully!', ]; } ``` ### 3. Handling CORS If you're accessing the API from a different origin (e.g., React app running on a different port or domain from your Yii2 backend), ensure that your Yii2 application has CORS configured correctly. In your Yii2 config: ```php 'reponse' => [ // Other components... 'on beforeResponse' => function ($event) { Yii::$app->response->headers->add('Access-Control-Allow-Origin', '*'); // Adjust with actual origin in production Yii::$app->response->headers->add('Access-Control-Allow-Methods', 'GET, POST, OPTIONS'); Yii::$app->response->headers->add('Access-Control-Allow-Headers', 'X-CSRF-Token, Content-Type'); }, ], ``` ### 4. Debugging - Make sure your CSRF token is not empty or undefined before making the request in the React app. - Use the browser's developer tools to inspect the request and response headers to ensure the token is being sent correctly. - Log the CSRF token being sent from the React app and check that the Yii2 logs show the same when validating the token. LLM: In Yii2, the CSRF token is usually sent with the header `X-CSRF-Token` when submitting AJAX requests. However, depending on how the server is configured or the middleware being used (if any), the server might interpret it differently. The header you see, `HTTP_X_CSRF_TOKEN`, is a PHP superglobal that represents the header received in the HTTP request. In PHP, server variables often start with `HTTP_` and use underscores instead of hyphens. When you access headers in Yii2 or in PHP, this is the convention that applies, and the framework translates the incoming HTTP headers accordingly. ### How to Handle CSRF Tokens Correctly 1. **Setting the Header in React**: Ensure that you are sending the CSRF token with the correct header in your React application. You are doing this correctly in your `handleSubmit` function by using `X-CSRF-Token` as the header key: ```javascript const response = await axios.post('http://localhost:8080/api/submit-form', { name, email, message }, { headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json', 'X-CSRF-Token': csrfToken // Sending as X-CSRF-Token }, }); ``` 2. **Validating the CSRF Token in Yii2**: In your Yii2 action, you can validate the CSRF token sent in the `X-CSRF-Token` header by accessing it through `Yii::$app->request->headers`. Here’s how you can do it: ```php public function actionSubmitForm(): Array { \Yii::$app->response->format = Response::FORMAT_JSON; $request = Yii::$app->request; \Yii::info('Received POST data: ' . json_encode($request->post()), __METHOD__); // Validate CSRF token from the headers if (!$request->validateCsrfToken($request->headers->get('X-CSRF-Token'))) { \Yii::error('Invalid CSRF token: ' . json_encode($request->headers->get('X-CSRF-Token')), __METHOD__); return [ 'status' => 'error', 'message' => 'Invalid CSRF Token', ]; } $name = $request->post('name'); $email = $request->post('email'); $message = $request->post('message'); \Yii::info("Form submission data - Name: $name, Email: $email, Message: $message", __METHOD__); // Here you can save the data or perform your success logic return [ 'status' => 'success', 'message' => 'Your message has been sent successfully!', ]; } ``` ### Summary - Use `X-CSRF-Token` in your React app when setting the header. - Access the CSRF token in your Yii2 backend using `$request->headers->get('X-CSRF-Token')` to validate it. - If you see `HTTP_X_CSRF_TOKEN` in the server variables, it is simply the name the server uses to reference the `X-CSRF-Token` header. As a developer, use the `X-CSRF-Token` in your requests for clarity, and always read it from the headers in your Yii2 application when validating. By following these practices, you should be able to correctly manage CSRF protection between your React frontend and Yii2 backend.

Example of a controller action that returns the CSRF token: ```php public function actionGetCsrf() { return [ 'csrfParam' => Yii::$app->request->csrfParam, 'csrfToken' => Yii::$app->request->csrfToken, ]; } ``` 2. **Fetching the CSRF Token in React**: - Ensure your React app is correctly fetching the CSRF token and setting it in the state. Here’s how your `useEffect` should look: ```javascript useEffect(() => { const fetchCsrfToken = async () => { try { const response = await axios.get('http://localhost:8080/api/csrf-token'); const data = response.data; if (data.csrfParam && data.csrfToken) { setCsrfTokenName(data.csrfParam); setCsrfToken(data.csrfToken); } else { throw new Error('CSRF token and param are not received'); } } catch (error) { console.error('Error fetching CSRF token:', error); setError('Failed to fetch CSRF token. Please try again.'); } }; fetchCsrfToken(); }, []); ``` 3. **Submitting the Form with CSRF Token**: - When you submit the form (in your `handleSubmit` function), ensure you are sending the CSRF token correctly within the headers. Example of how the headers should look: ```javascript const response = await axios.post('http://localhost:8080/api/submit-form', { name, email, message }, { headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json', 'X-CSRF-Token': csrfToken // Ensure this is sent in the request headers }, }); ``` 4. **Validating CSRF Token in Yii2**: - Check your Yii2 action where you validate the CSRF token. Make sure you are retrieving the token correctly from the request headers. This should look something like: ```php public function actionSubmitForm(): Array { \Yii::$app->response->format = Response::FORMAT_JSON; $request = Yii::$app->request; // Validate CSRF token from the headers if (!$request->validateCsrfToken($request->headers->get('X-CSRF-Token'))) { \Yii::error('Invalid CSRF token: ' . json_encode($request->headers->get('X-CSRF-Token')), __METHOD__); return [ 'status' => 'error', 'message' => 'Invalid CSRF Token', ]; } // The rest of your logic... } ``` 5. **Browser Console Debugging**: - Check your browser’s developer console (F12) under the Network tab to ensure that the `X-CSRF-Token` header is being sent correctly in the request. - Ensure that the value being sent matches the CSRF token that the server is expecting. 6. **CORS Configuration (if applicable)**: - If your React app and Yii2 API are running on different domains or ports, make sure your Yii2 backend has the appropriate CORS headers set. It should allow the origin of your React app to access the server and send headers. Add this to your Yii2 config: ```php 'components' => [ ... 'response' => [ ... 'on beforeResponse' => function ($event) { Yii::$app->response->headers->add('Access-Control-Allow-Origin', '*'); // Ideally, use your actual domain Yii::$app->response->headers->add('Access-Control-Allow-Methods', 'GET, POST, OPTIONS'); Yii::$app->response->headers->add('Access-Control-Allow-Headers', 'X-CSRF-Token, Content-Type'); }, ], ... ], ``` ### Debugging Steps 1. **Log the CSRF Token**: Add debugging logs to see what CSRF token is being fetched and what is being sent: ```php \Yii::info("CSRF Token from headers: " . json_encode($request->headers->get('X-CSRF-Token')), __METHOD__); ``` 2. **Output the CSRF Token Returned**: You might want to output the CSRF token returned by the `getCsrf` endpoint in the React console to verify that the token received from the server is the same as expected: ```javascript console.log('CSRF Token Value:', data.csrfToken); ``` 3. **Test in Postman**: You can test your Yii2 API with a tool like Postman. Manually set the `X-CSRF-Token` header in Postman to see if you can successfully validate the CSRF token. ### Conclusion Following these guidelines and checks should help you resolve the "Invalid CSRF Token" issue. Ensure that both the CSRF token retrieval and submission processes are correctly implemented and that the values being sent and validated match as expected.

You’re already doing this correctly with `Access-Control-Request-Headers`. 3. **Handling OPTIONS Requests**: Make sure your Yii2 application properly handles `OPTIONS` requests, which are typically sent before `POST` requests, especially for CORS. You should ensure that your CSRF validation logic only applies to `POST` requests and not `OPTIONS`. 4. **Response Headers**: Ensure that your Yii2 application sends appropriate CORS headers in the response. The CORS filter should handle this automatically if configured correctly, but you can add log messages to see what headers are getting sent in your responses. 5. **Testing with Browser Developer Tools**: Use the developer tools in your browser (usually F12) to check the network requests: - Confirm that the `X-CSRF-Token` header is included in your requests and matches the expected value. - Ensure that any `OPTIONS` preflight requests return a status of `200` and include the appropriate CORS headers. - Check the response headers for your `POST` request to confirm that they include `Access-Control-Allow-Origin`, `Access-Control-Allow-Headers`, and `Access-Control-Allow-Methods`. ### CSRF Token Sending in Fetch API Ensure that in your React application, when you're making requests to the Yii2 API, you're sending your CSRF token in the request as per the implementation I've mentioned earlier: ```javascript axios.post('http://localhost:8080/api/submit-form', { name, email, message }, { headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json', 'X-CSRF-Token': csrfToken // This should be the correct value fetched initially } }); ``` ### Debugging - If you are still getting "Invalid CSRF Token," log the received CSRF token in both the React app when fetched and in Yii2 when validating to ensure they match. You can do this with: - In React: `console.log("CSRF Token:", csrfToken);` - In Yii2: ```php \Yii::info("CSRF Token Received: " . json_encode($request->headers->get('X-CSRF-Token')), __METHOD__); ``` - Double-check that the values used for CSRF tokens and parameters in React and Yii2 match perfectly. ### Conclusion By ensuring that your CORS and CSRF configurations are set up correctly, and by logging any discrepancies in values when testing, you should be able to resolve the "Invalid CSRF Token" error. If issues persist, please check the browser session to see if any caching or cookie issues may be causing old tokens to be used.
USER: do i need to set anything within the web.php for session?

LLM: In a Yii2 application, session management is typically handled automatically by Yii2, but there are a few configurations you can set in `config/web.php` to ensure sessions are managed according to your specific needs. Here’s a comprehensive look at what you may want to configure regarding sessions: ### Basic Session Configuration By default, Yii2 uses PHP's native session handling, but you can customize it in the configuration file: ```php 'components' => [ 'session' => [ 'class' => 'yii\web\Session', 'timeout' => 1440, // Session timeout in minutes; default is 1440 (24 hours) // Optional settings: 'name' => 'YOUR_SESSION_NAME', // Set a custom session name // 'cookieParams' can be used to set cookie parameters 'cookieParams' => [ 'lifetime' => 0, // 0 means until the browser is closed 'path' => '/', 'domain' => '', // Domain for the cookie 'secure' => false, // Set to true if using HTTPS 'httponly' => true, 'samesite' => 'Lax', // Use 'Strict' or 'None' based on your needs ], ], // Other components... ], ``` ### CSRF Token Configuration While the session component is responsible for maintaining session data, you should ensure that the CSRF functionality is aligned with your session settings. ```php 'request' => [ 'enableCsrfValidation' => true, // CSRF validation enabled 'csrfParam' => '_csrf', // Default CSRF parameter name ], ``` ### Customizing Session Storage (Optional) If you want to change how your sessions are stored (e.g., using a database instead of files), you can create a component that extends the Yii2 `Session` class and override the `readSession` and `writeSession` methods to use a database. ### CORS and Sessions Ensure that session cookies (when sent by the client) can be handled correctly with CORS. If you are using credentials (e.g., cookies for session management) in your CORS settings, ensure that you allow credentials in the CORS filter and use the correct flags: ```php 'corsFilter' => [ 'class' => Cors::class, 'cors' => [ 'Origin' => ['http://localhost:3000'], 'Access-Control-Request-Method' => ['GET', 'POST', 'OPTIONS'], 'Access-Control-Request-Headers' => ['X-CSRF-Token', 'Content-Type'], 'Access-Control-Allow-Credentials' => true, // Allow credentials 'Access-Control-Max-Age' => 3600, ], ], ``` ### Testing Sessions To ensure sessions are working correctly: - Log information to see if sessions are being created and maintained between requests. - Print the session variables before and after submission in your Yii2 actions. Example: ```php Yii::$app->session->set('someKey', 'someValue'); Yii::info('Session: ' . json_encode(Yii::$app->session->getAllFlash()), __METHOD__); ``` ### Conclusion You don't **need** to set up much for session management beyond the default configuration in Yii2. However, adjusting the timeout, session parameters, and ensuring proper handling of CSRF tokens is essential to secure your application. If you're using CORS and credentials (session cookies), make sure your CORS configuration allows them. If you encounter specific issues related to sessions later, you can also monitor PHP error logs to diagnose potential problems. Always ensure that sessions are being maintained correctly across requests by logging necessary debug information.

This issue can arise from various reasons. Let's explore some possible causes and solutions: ### Possible Causes and Solutions 1. **Mismatched CSRF Token**: - The CSRF token used to validate the request must match the one that was stored in the user's session. Ensure that the CSRF token is being fetched correctly in your React app and that it hasn't changed between fetch and submission. - You should log the CSRF token when fetched and ensure it matches during handling. 2. **Session Management**: - Verify that session management is working properly in your application. The CSRF token is typically stored in a user session and tied to the unique user session ID. - You might want to log session variables right before validating the CSRF token in your Yii2 action: ```php Yii::info('Current Session Data: ' . json_encode(Yii::$app->session->getAll()), __METHOD__); ``` 3. **Testing CSRF Token Retrieval**: To isolate the problem: - Use Postman or a similar tool to manually set the `X-CSRF-Token` header and see if you can submit the form successfully. - Send a request to your CSRF token endpoint and make sure the token value received is the same one you are sending in the body of the actual POST request. 4. **Correct Token Retrieval**: - Ensure that the React application is getting and storing the CSRF token correctly. Log it right after fetching to confirm it's what you expect. 5. **Handling CORS**: - Given that you have CORS enabled, ensure Cookies are also properly set up. Sometimes the browser may not send the Cookies (including the session cookie) in CORS requests. - Ensure the `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials` is set to `true` in your CORS configuration and that your web browser can handle the cookies: ```php 'Access-Control-Allow-Credentials' => true, ``` Also, on the React side, make sure credentials are included in your Axios requests: ```javascript const response = await axios.post('http://localhost:8080/api/submit-form', { name, email, message }, { headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json', 'X-CSRF-Token': csrfToken }, withCredentials: true, // This is crucial for sending cookies in CORS requests }); ``` 6. **Debugging CSRF Outcomes**: - Log the expected CSRF token from the session before validating it and compare it to the incoming token: ```php Yii::info('Expected CSRF Token: ' . Yii::$app->request->csrfToken, __METHOD__); ``` ### Summary To summarize, ensure that: - The CSRF token is correctly fetched and not altered at any point. - The session is correctly established and not timing out or being cleared. - Credentials are sent with the request. - Perform manual testing with tools like Postman to confirm behavior outside of React. After going through this debugging checklist, you should be able to identify the root cause of the "Invalid CSRF Token" error. If issues persist, consider providing more logs or details on session handling and CSRF token generation for a more specific investigation.
USER: if im looking for 'X_CSRF_TOKEN' but the return is being changed to 'HTTP_X_CSRF_TOKEN' would this not be a issue?

LLM: Yes, that could be a potential source of confusion when working with headers in PHP and Yii2. ### Understanding Header Naming 1. **Header Format**: In PHP, the server variables you access have a specific naming convention: - HTTP headers that are received by the server are prefixed with `HTTP_` and underscores are used instead of dashes. - For example, if you're looking for the `X-CSRF-Token` header sent by the client, it will be accessible in your PHP application as `HTTP_X_CSRF_TOKEN`. 2. **Accessing the CSRF Token**: When checking for the CSRF token in Yii2, you should retrieve it from the headers using the appropriate method. You can do this as follows: ```php $csrfToken = Yii::$app->request->headers->get('X-CSRF-Token'); // Header name should be used with dashes ``` ### CSRF Token Validation When validating the CSRF token, make sure you are actually checking against the expected header value. Your code for validating should look something like this: ```php public function actionSubmitForm(): Array { \Yii::$app->response->format = Response::FORMAT_JSON; $request = Yii::$app->request; // Get the CSRF token from the header $csrfTokenFromHeader = $request->headers->get('X-CSRF-Token'); // Log for debugging \Yii::info("CSRF Token from headers: " . json_encode($csrfTokenFromHeader), __METHOD__); \Yii::info("Expected CSRF Token: " . Yii::$app->request->csrfToken, __METHOD__); // Validate CSRF token if (!$request->validateCsrfToken($csrfTokenFromHeader)) { \Yii::error('Invalid CSRF token: ' . json_encode($csrfTokenFromHeader), __METHOD__); return [ 'status' => 'error', 'message' => 'Invalid CSRF Token', ]; } // Continue with processing the form... } ``` ### Summary 1. **Use the Correct Header Name**: When you send a request with `X-CSRF-Token`, it should be accessed as `X-CSRF-Token` in your PHP code. 2. **PHP Superglobals**: Accessing headers in PHP, you will usually use `$_SERVER` which includes the `HTTP_` prefix as a way to access these headers. However, Yii2 provides a cleaner abstraction through the `request` object to access headers without needing to think about the manual details. 3. **No Need to Modify**: If you are using Yii2's `HttpRequest`, you don't need to worry about the `HTTP_` prefix. Just use Yii's methods to access headers directly with their original names. By properly accessing the header and validating it against what Yii2 expects, you should be able to resolve any confusion related to header naming and CSRF token validation.

LLM: Given the detailed logs you've provided, a few things merit investigation to understand why you are encountering the "Invalid CSRF Token" error, even though it appears that all expected headers and cookies are being set. ### Key Observations and Steps to Diagnose 1. **CSRF Token and Cookie Mismatch**: - In your logs, you can see the CSRF token coming from the headers (`HTTP_X_CSRF_TOKEN`) is: ``` D5psW6tMwmnaxAb3AGBsoERDfNo6pjz8BhJ2_X3RFdY24AU-0hyhM72Dfr5zVjbHJxEbqVf_RLpJUAy_KYZ0rw== ``` - The cookie `_csrf` contains a different (and likely older) token: ``` _csrf=acd6f461d7cad8b26ae4461346ebd5bedc8b79a4de1cd09eb05df1c5d4de92f8a:2:{i:0;s:5:"_csrf";i:1;s:32:"VBJDr1j_ahpTi0GeKmAYVo7jiXRAV7Rc";} ``` The token in the cookie does not match the one being sent in the request. This indicates that the CSRF token being sent in the request might be outdated. 2. **Steps to Ensure CSRF Token Consistency**: - **Fetch the Token**: Ensure your frontend fetches the CSRF token correctly before each form submission. When you call the endpoint to fetch the CSRF token, you should not be using stale tokens. Always ensure the token is up-to-date. - If you are fetching the CSRF token on component mount, ensure that any action that can change the session and possibly the CSRF token (like logging in, etc.) also updates the token. - To ensure that you have the latest token, re-fetch the CSRF token each time right before the form submission. 3. **Debugging the Token Handling**: - Add logs in the Yii2 controller to display the expected CSRF token fetched from the session right before validation: ```php Yii::info('Expected CSRF Token (from session): ' . Yii::$app->request->csrfToken, __METHOD__); ``` 4. **CORS Settings and Cookies**: - Make sure that the `withCredentials: true` setting is enabled whenever you send requests that require session cookies, as mentioned earlier in your Axios request. This ensures cookies (including the CSRF token cookie) are sent with the request. For instance: ```javascript const response = await axios.post('http://localhost:8080/api/submit-form', { name, email, message, }, { headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json', 'X-CSRF-Token': csrfToken, // the current token from state }, withCredentials: true, // this is crucial }); ``` 5. **Session Management**: - Verify that the session handling is functioning correctly, including cookie settings such as the path and domain. You want to ensure cookies are sent for requests from your React app to the Yii2 API. - If you’re running on localhost, you might want to ensure you’re accessing both the React app and the API under the same protocol (HTTP or HTTPS) and same base URL. 6. **Ensure No Manual Token Changes**: - Make sure there are no places in your code that manually set or modify the CSRF token before submission. ### Conclusion Given your setup: - Make sure you are consistently fetching and sending the most recent CSRF token. - Double-check your cookie handling in terms of both the frontend and backend settings. - Confirm that both your frontend and backend are not altering or using stale tokens. By methodically checking through each of these suggestions, you should be able to resolve the issue with the CSRF token validation. If problems persist, consider using debugging tools or libraries that can help trace HTTP traffic to further understand where the token mismatches occur.

LLM: Вот пример кода для создания панели с пятью кнопками в Blender, при нажатии на которые в консоль будет выводиться имя кнопки: ```python import bpy class SimplePanel(bpy.types.Panel): bl_idname = "OBJECT_PT_simple_panel" bl_label = "Simple Panel" bl_space_type = "VIEW_3D" bl_region_type = "UI" bl_category = "My Addon" def draw(self, context): layout = self.layout # Создаем первую кнопку row = layout.row() row.operator("object.rotate", text="Rotate", icon="OBJECT_ORIGIN").name = "Rotate" # Создаем вторую кнопку row = layout.row() row.operator("object.scale", text="Scale", icon="MAN_SCALE").name = "Scale" # Создаем третью кнопку row = layout.row() row.operator("object.translate", text="Translate", icon="MANIPUL").name = "Translate" # Создаем четвертую кнопку row = layout.row() row.operator("object.duplicate", text="Duplicate", icon="DUPLICATE").name = "Duplicate" # Создаем пятую кнопку row = layout.row() row.operator("object.delete", text="Delete", icon="TRASH").name = "Delete" def print_button_name(self, context): print(self.name) def register(): bpy.utils.register_class(SimplePanel) bpy.types.Operator.name = bpy.props.StringProperty() bpy.types.Operator.execute = print_button_name def unregister(): bpy.utils.unregister_class(SimplePanel) del bpy.types.Operator.name del bpy.types.Operator.execute if __name__ == "__main__": register() ``` После сохранения файла и установки его как аддон в Blender, вы найдете созданную панель "Simple Panel" в меню "View3D" в разделе "My Addon". При нажатии на кнопки на панели, имя каждой кнопки будет напечатано в консоль.

LLM: Вот пример кода для создания панели с одной кнопкой в Blender, при нажатии на которую в консоль будет выводиться строка "123": ```python import bpy class SimplePanel(bpy.types.Panel): bl_idname = "OBJECT_PT_simple_panel" bl_label = "Simple Panel" bl_space_type = "VIEW_3D" bl_region_type = "UI" bl_category = "My Addon" def draw(self, context): layout = self.layout # Создаем кнопку row = layout.row() row.operator("object.my_operator", text="Print", icon="NONE") class OBJECT_OT_my_operator(bpy.types.Operator): bl_idname = "object.my_operator" bl_label = "Print 123" def execute(self, context): print("123") return {'FINISHED'} def register(): bpy.utils.register_class(SimplePanel) bpy.utils.register_class(OBJECT_OT_my_operator) def unregister(): bpy.utils.unregister_class(SimplePanel) bpy.utils.unregister_class(OBJECT_OT_my_operator) if __name__ == "__main__": register() ``` После сохранения файла и его установки как аддона в Blender, вы найдете созданную панель "Simple Panel" в меню "View3D" в разделе "My Addon". При нажатии на кнопку на панели в консоль будет выводиться строка "123".
